title: Git command overview
summary: A human-friendly map of the Git commands you use most often: create, inspect, stage, commit, branch, sync, undo, and recover.
content:
## How to think about Git

Git is a history tracker for your code. It watches a folder, records snapshots as commits, and lets you move between versions safely.

## The four everyday areas

Working tree: the files you are editing right now.

Staging area: the files selected for the next commit.

Local repository: your committed history on your machine.

Remote repository: shared history on GitHub, GitLab, Azure Repos, or another server.

## Most common command flow

You edit files, check status, stage changes, commit them, then push the commit to a remote branch.

## Core commands by purpose

Start: `git init`, `git clone`.

Inspect: `git status`, `git diff`, `git log`, `git show`.

Save: `git add`, `git commit`.

Branch: `git branch`, `git switch`, `git checkout`.

Sync: `git fetch`, `git pull`, `git push`.

Undo: `git restore`, `git reset`, `git revert`.

Recover: `git reflog`, `git stash`.

## Interview line

Git is not just for saving code. It is how teams review, share, rollback, and understand changes.

code_examples.title: Daily flow
code_examples.code:
git status
git add src/app.js
git commit -m "Add search UI"
git push origin feature/search-ui

title: Setup and configuration
summary: Configure your name, email, default branch, editor, line endings, and shortcuts before you start using Git heavily.
content:
## Why setup matters

Every commit records an author name and email. If your Git identity is wrong, your commits may show under the wrong account in GitHub or GitLab.

## Global vs local config

Global config applies to all repositories on your machine. Local config applies only to the current repository.

## Important settings

Set your name and email, choose your default branch name, and configure useful aliases. On Windows, line ending settings can also matter because teams may use different operating systems.

## Helpful commands

`git config --list` shows active config.

`git config --global user.name "Your Name"` sets your name globally.

`git config --global user.email "you@example.com"` sets your email globally.

`git config --global init.defaultBranch main` makes new repos start with `main`.

## Interview line

Before collaborating, make sure Git identity and branch defaults are configured correctly.

code_examples.title: Basic setup
code_examples.code:
git config --global user.name "Ada Lovelace"
git config --global user.email "ada@example.com"
git config --global init.defaultBranch main
git config --list

code_examples.title: Useful aliases
code_examples.code:
git config --global alias.st status
git config --global alias.co checkout
git config --global alias.br branch
git config --global alias.lg "log --oneline --graph --decorate --all"

title: init and clone
summary: `git init` creates a new repository in an existing folder; `git clone` copies an existing remote repository.
content:
## git init

Use `git init` when you already have a folder and want Git to start tracking it. It creates a hidden `.git` folder that stores repository history.

## git clone

Use `git clone` when the repository already exists somewhere else. Clone downloads the files and the Git history.

## Human difference

`init` means: start history here.

`clone` means: bring an existing history here.

## Common mistake

Do not run `git init` inside a folder that is already a clone unless you know exactly what you are doing. You may create nested repositories and confuse tooling.

code_examples.title: Start a new repository
code_examples.code:
mkdir my-app
cd my-app
git init

code_examples.title: Clone an existing repository
code_examples.code:
git clone https://github.com/org/project.git
cd project

title: status, add, and commit
summary: `git status` tells you what changed, `git add` stages files, and `git commit` saves a snapshot.
content:
## git status

This is the command you should run constantly. It tells you which files changed, which files are staged, and which branch you are on.

## git add

`git add` moves changes into the staging area. Staging means: these are the exact changes I want in my next commit.

## git commit

`git commit` saves the staged changes as a named point in history. A good commit message explains the reason for the change, not just the file names.

## Human workflow

Think of staging like packing a box. Committing is sealing the box and writing a label on it.

## Common mistake

Do not blindly run `git add .` if you have not checked status. You may accidentally commit logs, env files, build output, or unrelated edits.

code_examples.title: Stage and commit selected files
code_examples.code:
git status
git add src/components/Search.tsx
git add src/lib/search.ts
git commit -m "Add global search"

code_examples.title: Stage everything intentionally
code_examples.code:
git status
git add .
git commit -m "Update dashboard layout"

title: diff, log, and show
summary: Use these commands to inspect what changed before committing and understand existing history.
content:
## git diff

`git diff` shows unstaged changes. `git diff --staged` shows what is already staged for the next commit.

## git log

`git log` shows commit history. Use `--oneline` for a compact view and `--graph` to see branches and merges visually.

## git show

`git show` displays one commit: message, metadata, and file changes.

## Human habit

Before committing, run `git diff` and `git diff --staged`. It is like proofreading your change before sharing it.

## Common mistake

If `git diff` shows nothing, it might mean your changes are already staged. Check `git diff --staged`.

code_examples.title: Inspect changes
code_examples.code:
git diff
git diff --staged

code_examples.title: Read history
code_examples.code:
git log --oneline --graph --decorate --all
git show HEAD
git show abc123

title: branch, switch, and checkout
summary: Branches let you work on features separately; `git switch` moves between branches and `git branch` manages them.
content:
## What a branch is

A branch is a movable name pointing to a commit. When you commit on a branch, that branch moves forward.

## git branch

Use `git branch` to list, create, rename, and delete branches.

## git switch

Use `git switch` to move between branches. It is newer and clearer than using `checkout` for everything.

## git checkout

`git checkout` is older and can switch branches, restore files, or check out commits. It still appears in many tutorials, but `switch` and `restore` are clearer for daily work.

## Common mistake

Always check your current branch before committing. Many accidental commits happen on `main` because someone forgot to switch.

code_examples.title: Create and switch branches
code_examples.code:
git branch
git switch -c feature/login
git switch main

code_examples.title: Rename and delete branches
code_examples.code:
git branch -m old-name new-name
git branch -d feature/login

title: remote, fetch, pull, and push
summary: These commands connect your local repo with shared remote repositories and move commits between them.
content:
## git remote

A remote is a saved shortcut to another repository, usually named `origin`. It points to GitHub, GitLab, Azure Repos, or another Git server.

## git fetch

`git fetch` downloads remote history but does not change your working branch. It is a safe way to see what changed remotely.

## git pull

`git pull` downloads remote changes and immediately integrates them into your current branch. It is basically fetch plus merge, or fetch plus rebase depending on config.

## git push

`git push` uploads your local commits to a remote branch.

## Human rule

Fetch is look. Pull is bring into my branch. Push is share my commits.

code_examples.title: Check and sync remotes
code_examples.code:
git remote -v
git fetch origin
git pull origin main
git push origin feature/login

code_examples.title: Set upstream on first push
code_examples.code: git push -u origin feature/login

title: merge and rebase
summary: Merge combines branches with a merge commit; rebase moves your commits on top of another branch for a linear history.
content:
## git merge

Merge combines another branch into your current branch. It preserves the fact that two lines of work came together.

## git rebase

Rebase takes your commits and replays them on top of another branch. It can make history cleaner and easier to read.

## Which one should you use?

Use merge when you want to preserve branch history or when team policy prefers merge commits.

Use rebase to update your feature branch with the latest main branch before opening or updating a pull request.

## Important safety rule

Do not rebase shared branches unless your team agrees. Rebasing rewrites commit history, which can confuse teammates who already pulled that branch.

code_examples.title: Merge main into current branch
code_examples.code:
git switch feature/login
git fetch origin
git merge origin/main

code_examples.title: Rebase feature branch on main
code_examples.code:
git switch feature/login
git fetch origin
git rebase origin/main

title: Conflict resolution
summary: Conflicts happen when Git cannot automatically combine changes; you fix the files, mark them resolved, and continue.
content:
## Why conflicts happen

A conflict happens when two branches change the same part of a file differently, or when one branch deletes a file another branch edits.

## What Git does

Git stops and marks the conflicting file. You open the file, choose the correct final content, remove conflict markers, then stage the resolved file.

## Merge conflict flow

During a merge, fix the file, run tests if needed, stage it, then commit the merge.

## Rebase conflict flow

During a rebase, fix the file, stage it, then run `git rebase --continue`.

## Human advice

Do not panic. A conflict is Git asking you to make a decision it cannot safely make alone.

code_examples.title: Resolve during merge
code_examples.code:
git status
# edit conflicted files
git add src/app.js
git commit

code_examples.title: Resolve during rebase
code_examples.code:
git status
# edit conflicted files
git add src/app.js
git rebase --continue

code_examples.title: Abort if needed
code_examples.code:
git merge --abort
git rebase --abort

title: restore, reset, and revert
summary: These are Git's main undo tools: restore files, move branch pointers, or create a new commit that reverses an old one.
content:
## git restore

Use `git restore` to discard changes in files or unstage files. It is usually the safest daily undo command.

## git reset

Use `git reset` to move the current branch pointer. `--soft` keeps changes staged, `--mixed` keeps changes unstaged, and `--hard` discards changes. Be very careful with `--hard`.

## git revert

Use `git revert` when the commit is already shared. It creates a new commit that undoes another commit, so history stays safe for teammates.

## Simple rule

Private local commit? Reset can be okay.

Shared commit? Prefer revert.

Uncommitted file change? Use restore.

## Safety warning

`git reset --hard` can delete local work. Run `git status` first and make sure you really want to throw changes away.

code_examples.title: Restore file changes
code_examples.code:
git restore src/app.js
git restore --staged src/app.js

code_examples.title: Reset last local commit but keep changes
code_examples.code: git reset --soft HEAD~1

code_examples.title: Revert a shared commit
code_examples.code: git revert abc123

title: stash
summary: `git stash` temporarily shelves uncommitted changes so you can switch branches or pull cleanly.
content:
## What stash does

Stash is like putting your unfinished work into a temporary drawer. Your working tree becomes clean, and you can bring the changes back later.

## When to use it

Use stash when you are halfway through work and need to switch branches, pull changes, or quickly fix something else.

## pop vs apply

`git stash pop` applies the stash and removes it from the stash list.

`git stash apply` applies the stash but keeps it saved.

## Common mistake

Do not use stash as long-term storage. If work matters, commit it on a branch instead.

code_examples.title: Save and restore stash
code_examples.code:
git stash push -m "WIP login form"
git stash list
git stash pop

code_examples.title: Apply a specific stash

title: tags and releases
summary: Tags are named pointers to commits, commonly used for releases like `v1.0.0`.
content:
## What a tag is

A tag is a stable name for a specific commit. Unlike a branch, a tag usually does not move.

## Lightweight vs annotated tags

A lightweight tag is just a pointer. An annotated tag includes metadata like tagger, date, and message.

## Why teams use tags

Tags mark releases, deployment points, rollback targets, and important milestones.

## Common habit

Use semantic version tags like `v1.2.0` when releasing packages or applications.

code_examples.title: Create and push tags
code_examples.code:
git tag v1.0.0
git push origin v1.0.0

code_examples.title: Annotated tag
code_examples.code:
git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "Release version 1.0.0"
git push origin v1.0.0

code_examples.title: List tags
code_examples.code:
git tag
git show v1.0.0

title: reflog and recovery
summary: `git reflog` shows where your branch and HEAD have recently been, which can help recover lost commits.
content:
## What reflog is

Reflog is Git's local safety journal. It records movements of HEAD and branches on your machine.

## Why it saves people

If you reset, rebase, switch branches, or accidentally lose a commit, reflog often remembers where that commit was.

## Important detail

Reflog is local. It is not shared with the remote repository. Your teammate's reflog is not your reflog.

## Human advice

When you think you lost work, stop making more changes and check reflog before trying random commands.

code_examples.title: Recover a commit
code_examples.code:
git reflog
# find the commit hash you want
git switch -c recovered-work abc123

code_examples.title: Move back to previous HEAD
code_examples.code:
git reflog
git reset --hard HEAD@{1}

title: cherry-pick
summary: `git cherry-pick` copies one or more specific commits from another branch onto your current branch.
content:
## What cherry-pick does

Cherry-pick takes a commit from somewhere else and applies its changes to your current branch as a new commit.

## When to use it

Use it when you need one fix from another branch without merging the whole branch. This is common for hotfixes.

## Common example

A bug fix was committed on `main`, and you need the same fix on a release branch. Cherry-pick just that commit.

## Caution

Do not overuse cherry-pick as a replacement for a clean branch strategy. It can duplicate commits across history and make tracking changes harder.

code_examples.title: Cherry-pick one commit
code_examples.code:
git switch release/1.0
git cherry-pick abc123

code_examples.title: Cherry-pick multiple commits
code_examples.code: git cherry-pick abc123 def456

title: clean, rm, and mv
summary: These commands remove untracked files, delete tracked files, and move or rename tracked files.
content:
## git clean

`git clean` removes untracked files. This can delete files permanently, so always preview first with `-n`.

## git rm

`git rm` removes a tracked file and stages that removal for the next commit.

## git mv

`git mv` renames or moves a tracked file and stages the change.

## Simple rule

Tracked file? Use `git rm` or `git mv`.

Untracked generated junk? Use `git clean`, carefully.

## Safety warning

Run `git clean -n` before `git clean -fd`. Preview first, delete second.

code_examples.title: Preview and clean untracked files
code_examples.code:
git clean -n
git clean -fd

code_examples.title: Remove or move tracked files
code_examples.code:
git rm old-file.js
git mv old-name.js new-name.js
git commit -m "Rename utility file"

title: .gitignore
summary: A `.gitignore` file tells Git which untracked files should not be committed.
content:
## What .gitignore does

`.gitignore` prevents unwanted untracked files from showing up in Git status. Common ignored files include dependencies, build output, logs, local env files, and editor folders.

## Important limitation

`.gitignore` does not automatically stop tracking a file that is already committed. If Git already tracks the file, you must remove it from the index.

## Common frontend ignores

`node_modules`, `.next`, `dist`, `build`, `.env.local`, log files, and cache folders are usually ignored.

## Interview line

`.gitignore` protects the repository from machine-specific and generated files.

code_examples.title: Example .gitignore
code_examples.code:
node_modules
.next
dist
build
.env.local
*.log

code_examples.title: Stop tracking an already tracked file
code_examples.code:
git rm --cached .env.local
git commit -m "Stop tracking local env file"

title: blame and bisect
summary: `git blame` explains who last changed each line; `git bisect` finds the commit that introduced a bug.
content:
## git blame

`git blame` shows the last commit that touched each line in a file. Use it to understand context, not to accuse people.

## git bisect

`git bisect` helps find the exact commit where a bug started. You mark one commit as good and another as bad, and Git checks commits in the middle until it finds the first bad one.

## Why this matters

In production debugging, knowing when a bug appeared often matters more than guessing from the current code.

## Human advice

Blame the change, not the person. Git history is for understanding, not finger-pointing.

code_examples.title: Inspect line history
code_examples.code:
git blame src/app.js
git show abc123

code_examples.title: Find a bad commit
code_examples.code:
git bisect start
git bisect bad
git bisect good v1.0.0
# test each checked-out commit
git bisect good
# or
git bisect bad
git bisect reset

title: worktree
summary: `git worktree` lets you check out multiple branches into separate folders from one repository.
content:
## What worktree solves

Sometimes you need to work on two branches at once without constantly stashing or switching. Worktrees let one repository have multiple working folders.

## Practical use

You can keep your feature branch open in one folder and create a second folder for a hotfix branch.

## Why it is better than cloning twice

Worktrees share the same repository storage, so they are lighter than separate clones.

## Common habit

Remove a worktree when you are done so stale folders do not pile up.

code_examples.title: Create and remove a worktree
code_examples.code:
git worktree add ../project-hotfix hotfix/login-bug
cd ../project-hotfix
# work normally
cd ../project
git worktree remove ../project-hotfix

code_examples.title: List worktrees
code_examples.code: git worktree list

title: submodules
summary: Git submodules let one repository include another repository at a specific commit.
content:
## What submodules are

A submodule is a Git repository inside another Git repository. The parent repo records the exact commit of the child repo.

## When teams use them

Submodules are used for shared libraries, vendor code, docs, or code that must stay in a separate repository.

## Why they can be painful

Submodules require extra commands. New developers often clone the parent repo and forget to initialize submodules.

## Human advice

Use submodules only when the separate repository boundary is truly needed. For many JavaScript projects, packages or monorepos are easier.

code_examples.title: Add and clone submodules
code_examples.code:
git submodule add https://github.com/org/shared-ui.git packages/shared-ui
git submodule update --init --recursive

code_examples.title: Clone with submodules
code_examples.code: git clone --recurse-submodules https://github.com/org/project.git

title: patch, apply, and archive
summary: Git can export changes as patch files, apply patch files, and archive repository snapshots.
content:
## git diff as a patch

A patch is a text file describing changes. You can create one from `git diff` and send it to someone without pushing a branch.

## git apply

`git apply` applies a patch to your working tree. It does not automatically create a commit.

## git format-patch and am

`git format-patch` exports commits as email-style patch files. `git am` applies them as commits.

## git archive

`git archive` exports a clean snapshot of files from a commit or branch, without the `.git` history.

## Practical use

These commands are less common in daily frontend work, but useful in open-source, restricted environments, and release packaging.

code_examples.title: Create and apply patch
code_examples.code:
git diff > fix.patch
git apply fix.patch

code_examples.title: Patch from commits
code_examples.code:
git format-patch -1 HEAD
git am 0001-my-change.patch

code_examples.title: Archive source
code_examples.code: git archive --format=zip --output=source.zip HEAD

title: maintenance, gc, and fsck
summary: Git has maintenance commands for cleaning internal storage and checking repository integrity.
content:
## git gc

`git gc` runs garbage collection. It packs loose objects and cleans internal repository data. Git usually runs this automatically when needed.

## git fsck

`git fsck` checks repository object integrity. You rarely need it, but it can help diagnose repository corruption.

## git count-objects

`git count-objects -v` shows storage details, useful when a repository is getting large.

## Daily reality

Most developers do not run these often. They are good to know when a repo becomes slow, huge, or suspiciously broken.

## Interview line

Git stores data as objects, and maintenance commands help inspect or clean that object database.

code_examples.title: Repository maintenance
code_examples.code:
git count-objects -v
git gc
git fsck
